_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Sumter County, AL. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. Sumter County (formed 1832 from Choctaw cession lands, seat at Livingston) has good record survival — no major courthouse fire. Records begin at county formation.

previous_courthouses.fate: Served the county's earliest years. Replaced as the county grew.
Survived the Civil War without significant damage. Replaced by the 1902 building.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire or war damage to courthouse
record_losses.description: Sumter County's courthouse has not experienced a catastrophic fire. Records run from the county's 1832 formation. As with all Black Belt counties, the critical gap is not lost records but absent records — enslaved people were not documented as persons in county records before 1865.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Sumter County's records survive from 1832. If {ancestor_name} was free and white, the paper trail is likely continuous. If {ancestor_name} was enslaved, the records exist only as property entries — estate inventories, slave schedules, and deed transfers — until the Freedmen's Bureau and post-1865 county records give formerly enslaved people their own documentary identity.
record_losses.source: Alabama Department of Archives and History. Guide to Alabama County Records.

deeds.repository: Sumter County Probate Office; ADAH microfilm

wills_estates.completeness: Good — estate inventories from near-formation are a critical source for enslaved families
wills_estates.gaps: Minor gaps in earliest loose papers.

marriages.completeness: Good for white marriages. Freedmen's marriages from 1865.
marriages.gaps: No known significant gaps in bound volumes.
marriages.repository: Sumter County Probate Office; ADAH; Freedmen's Bureau at NARA

vital_records.repository: Alabama Center for Health Statistics; ADAH

substitute_sources.description: Bureau records from the Livingston sub-district document formerly enslaved families.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for documenting Black families transitioning from slavery. Labor contracts link individuals to specific plantations.
substitute_sources.source: NARA, Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands, RG 105.

substitute_sources.description: Alabama's state normal school at Livingston (now the University of West Alabama) maintained enrollment records from 1835.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Enrollment and alumni records document families across the county. Teacher training records are particularly useful for identifying middle-class families.

substitute_sources.description: ADAH holds microfilm of Sumter County records, Confederate service records, and Reconstruction-era state records.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Backup repository for all county record types.

narrative_hooks: Sumter County's courthouse survived every decade since 1832 — {ancestor_name}'s deeds and wills almost certainly still exist, from Choctaw cession to the present.

Before 1865, {ancestor_name}'s enslaved ancestors appear in Sumter County estate inventories as names with ages and values — the cruel arithmetic of property law applied to human beings. After 1865, the Freedmen's Bureau records give those same people their own documentary voice.

Sumter County's records are intact, but the Black Belt's most important record gap is not lost documents — it is the systematic exclusion of enslaved people from the legal record as persons. The silence in the record IS the record of slavery.
Alabama vital registration began in 1908 — for {ancestor_name} born in Sumter County before that date, the census, the church roll, and the family Bible are the evidence.
source_references: Alabama Department of Archives and History. Guide to Alabama County Records.
National Archives and Records Administration. Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands, Record Group 105.
Works Progress Administration. Historical Records Survey: Alabama County Records. Montgomery, 1937–1942.
